coredata.dc:title: Drug release of the polyvinylpyrrolidone/carbon quantum dots/polyethylene glycol self-assembled Nanocarrier: Kinetics and the targeted breast cancer studies
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology

Pourmadadi, Mehrab
Yazdian, Fatemeh
Rashedi, Hamid
Rahdar, Abbas
Fathi-karkan, Sonia
Romanholo Ferreira, Luiz Fernando
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a new pH-responsive drug delivery system using a polyvinylpyrrolidone/polyethylene glycol/carbon quantum dots (PVP-PEG-CQDs) hydrogel nanocomposite was formulated. The novel platform exhibited good drug-loading (58 %) and encapsulation (92 %) efficiency and extended drug release for 96 h with selectively enhanced release under acidic tumor microenvironmental conditions (pH 5.4). Thorough characterization by FTIR, FESEM, DLS and zeta potential analysis established the structural integrity of the nanocomposite, nanoscale dimensions (average size: 75 nm), and excellent colloidal stability (zeta potential: −62.5 mV). In vitro biological assays showed improved anti-cancer activity against MCF-7 breast cancer cells compared to free DOX, with much greater cytotoxicity (p < 0.001) and a different cell death mechanism of apoptosis and necrosis. The pH-responsiveness and prolonged release profile of the nanocomposite effectively minimized non-specific toxicity without affecting therapeutic efficacy. These results position the PVP-PEG-CQDs system as a promising targeted therapy platform for improving the prognosis of breast cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doxorubicin
pH-responsive hydrogel
Polyvinylpyrrolidone
Sustained drug release
Nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectrum of synthesized CQDs, PVP, PVP-PEG, PVP-PEG-CQDs, and PVP-PEG-CQDs-DOX.
FESEM images of freeze-dried DOX-loaded hydrogel nanocomposites.
DLS analysis of DOX-loaded hydrogel nanocomposite. Zeta potential analysis of fabricated nano-system.
Release profile of DOX from PVP-PEG-CQDs nanocomposite in two different buffer media (pH = 5.4 and 7.4).
Plots of different types of drug release kinetic modeling.

After 72 h, free DOX, DOX-loaded PVP-PEG, PVP-PEG-CQDs-DOX, and PVP-PEG-CQDs without DOX killed MCF-7 cells. The mean and SEM from three duplicate experiments are provided. The differences between the control group, free DOX, PVP-PEG, and PVP-PEG-CQDs are significant (p < 0.001) (∗∗∗). Significant difference in DOX loading between PVP-PEG and PVP-PEG-CQDs (p < 0.001) (###).

MCF-7 flow cytometry findings. Free DOX (D), PVP/PEG/DOX (B), PVP/PEG/CQDs (C), and PVP/PEG/CQDs/DOX (E) were independently treated for 48 h. Control sample (A) was incubated untreated. The accompanying graphic compares the percentages of necrotic (Q1), late (Q2), early (Q3), and viable (Q4) cells in each treatment sample.
Entrapment and loading efficiency of different DDS used as DOX carrier.

head.ce:title: Drug release of the polyvinylpyrrolidone/carbon quantum dots/polyethylene glycol self-assembled Nanocarrier: Kinetics and the targeted breast cancer studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a new pH-responsive drug delivery system using a polyvinylpyrrolidone/polyethylene glycol/carbon quantum dots (PVP-PEG-CQDs) hydrogel nanocomposite was formulated. The novel platform exhibited good drug-loading (58 %) and encapsulation (92 %) efficiency and extended drug release for 96 h with selectively enhanced release under acidic tumor microenvironmental conditions (pH 5.4). Thorough characterization by FTIR, FESEM, DLS and zeta potential analysis established the structural integrity of the nanocomposite, nanoscale dimensions (average size: 75 nm), and excellent colloidal stability (zeta potential: −62.5 mV). In vitro biological assays showed improved anti-cancer activity against MCF-7 breast cancer cells compared to free DOX, with much greater cytotoxicity (p < 0.001) and a different cell death mechanism of apoptosis and necrosis. The pH-responsiveness and prolonged release profile of the nanocomposite effectively minimized non-specific toxicity without affecting therapeutic efficacy. These results position the PVP-PEG-CQDs system as a promising targeted therapy platform for improving the prognosis of breast cancer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is the second most common cause of death worldwide, responsible for approximately 9.6 million deaths across various types in 2018 [1]. Among these, breast cancer is one of the most commonly diagnosed malignancies globally and ranks as the second leading cause of cancer-related deaths in the United States, with more than 41,000 fatalities reported in 2019. Breast cancer is a significant global health issue, and conventional doxorubicin (DOX) chemotherapy is limited by systemic toxicity, premature drug degradation, and resistance development. To surmount these challenges [2,3].

The therapeutic approaches for breast cancer include surgical excision, hormone therapy, radiation, and chemotherapy, the latter being the most prevalent. Chemotherapy often entails several disadvantages, including significant toxicity, absence of targeted delivery, and harm to normal tissues, even at minimal doses. Moreover, late-stage cancers often resist chemotherapy, hence the interest in developing new drug delivery systems that would improve effectiveness and selectivity [4–8].

Doxorubicin (DOX), a recognized chemotherapeutic drug derived from Streptomyces peucetius subsp., attacks cancer cells via enzyme inhibition and DNA intercalation. Notwithstanding its effectiveness, DOX has obstacles including cardiotoxicity, hydrolytic instability, and fast enzymatic degradation, hence requiring sophisticated delivery strategies to alleviate these issues [9–11]. Moreover, the breast cancer cells develop DOX resistance due to events like downregulation of Lipocalin 2 (LCN2) and hence, emphasizing a new nano-sized platforms would help in overcoming the challenging hurdles besides increasing DOX uptake in cancer cells [12]. Hydrogels have emerged as a viable option in drug delivery systems due to their three-dimensional architecture, biocompatibility, and capacity to hold substantial water content.

pH-sensitive hydrogels deliver medications selectively by using the acidic tumor microenvironment, facilitating targeted therapy while minimizing adverse effects on healthy cells [13–15]. Incorporation of nanoparticles into hydrogel systems has proven effective in overcoming some limitations of hydrogels, such as insufficient homogeneity and limited mechanical strength, thus enhancing the encapsulation and stability of hydrophobic drugs [16]. In advanced drug delivery systems, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) are known for their outstanding features. PVP, due to its biocompatibility and pH-responsive nature, enables controlled release of the entrapped drug, while PEG offers enhanced biocompatibility, hydrophilicity, and prolongs circulation time, thus hindering the rapid removal from the bloodstream [17–20]. Carbon-based nano-materials like CNTs and hybrid nano structures with silicon and other substances have been investigated for different applications due to good properties [19,21,22].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with very small size, functional groups, and large surface area improve the loading efficiency and targeted distribution of hydrogels, which are thus particularly suitable for the cancer therapy and diagnosis applications [23–25]. This work prepared a pH-responsive double-emulsion hydrogel nanocomposite containing PVP, PEG, and CQDs (PVP-PEG-CQDs) to resolve the problems of DOX delivery. The engineered technology facilitates drug encapsulation, enables sustained release of drugs, and enhances the cytotoxicity toward MCF-7 breast cancer cells. The designed platform exhibited pH-dependent release behavior and significant cytotoxicity in comparison with free DOX, rendering it a potential candidate for targeted therapy against breast cancer. The new approach enhances medication retention at therapeutic levels while reducing systemic toxicity, representing a huge stride in the treatment of cancer.

The study used the MCF-7 human breast cancer cell line to evaluate the cytotoxic effects of a novel doxorubicin (DOX) delivery system. The cells were maintained in high-glucose Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) and supplemented with antibiotics from Gibco-Life Technologies. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and MTT reagent were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich, while cell culture plates and flasks were sourced from Corning, New York, USA. PBS was used for washing and solution preparation. The hydrogel nanocomposite was synthesized using key polymers from Sigma-Aldrich, including polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, molecular weight: 40 kDa), polyethylene glycol (PEG), and reagents from Sigma-Aldrich. Pure doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) was used as the active therapeutic compound. Span 80 surfactant and paraffin oil were used to form the double-emulsion system. All chemicals and materials used in the study were of analytical grade and used without additional purification.

The preparation of CQDs involved a systematic process starting with the use of di-ammonium hydrogen citrate as the primary carbon source. The carbon precursor was dissolved in distilled water to create a homogeneous solution, followed by the addition of MgCl2·6H2O as a catalyst and modifier. The mixture was stirred to ensure uniform distribution of reactants. NaOH was incorporated to stabilize the resulting nanoparticles, adjusting the pH and contributing to the formation of stable CQDs with uniform size and enhanced functional properties. The solution was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor, a sealed high-pressure vessel, for controlled thermal reactions. The reactor was heated to 150 °C and maintained for 24 h, facilitating the thermal decomposition of the carbon precursor. The mixture of magnesium chloride and sodium hydroxide supported the formation and stabilization of CQDs, influencing their size, structure, and optical behavior. The reactor was cooled to room temperature to prevent abrupt changes that could alter the structural integrity of the CQDs. The resulting solution was collected and prepared for downstream processes, ensuring the CQDs retained their desired properties for use in various fields, including bioimaging, sensing, and nanomedicine [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of double emulsion hydrogel nanocomposite

ce:section.ce:para: The hydrogel nanocomposite was prepared through a precise process to create a stable and effective drug delivery system. First, a 2 % w/v solution of PVP was prepared by dissolving 20 g of PVP in deionized water, stirring continuously for 30 min to achieve complete dissolution and homogeneity [27]. PEG was then incorporated into the PVP solution at a concentration of 1 % w/v, modifying the hydrophilicity and mechanical properties of the hydrogel. CQDs were added to the PVP-PEG solution at a final concentration of 0.1 % w/v, providing functional enhancements like improved optical properties and potential fluorescence for bioimaging applications [28]. The dispersion of CQDs was carefully managed to maintain uniformity and avoid aggregation. The therapeutic agent, DOX, was introduced dropwise, ensuring gradual and uniform distribution throughout the solution. The final concentration of DOX was adjusted to 7 μg/mL, making it suitable for targeted drug delivery while minimizing potential cytotoxic effects on surrounding tissues [29]. This resulted in a PVP-PEG-CQDs-DOX solution, which combined the polymer matrix, nanoparticles, and model drug. Glutaraldehyde was used as a crosslinking agent to establish the hydrogel's three-dimensional structure and provide stability for encapsulating both nanoparticles and DOX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of double emulsion hydrogel nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of a water-in-oil-in-water (w/o/w) double emulsion hydrogel nanocomposite involved a methodical process to encapsulate DOX within a crosslinked polymer network. The hydrophobic phase was prepared by dissolved Span 80 in paraffin oil, reducing interfacial tension between the aqueous and oil phases. The crosslinked hydrogel nanocomposite was added dropwise to the hydrophobic phase, resulting in a stable primary w/o emulsion. A hydrophilic phase was prepared by dissolving 1 % PVA in deionized water. The primary w/o emulsion was gradually introduced into the PVA solution, resulting in a double emulsion with a phase-separated structure. The hydrophobic layer, primarily composed of paraffin oil, was carefully removed to isolate the desired nanocomposite-rich phase. The remaining emulsion was centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 5 min to remove residual oil and unreacted components, ensuring a clean and uniform product. The purified nanocomposite was then freeze-dried to remove moisture and transform the material into a stable powder form suitable for storage and applications. This process preserved the structural and functional integrity of the hydrogel nanocomposite, resulting in a lightweight, porous material with enhanced stability.

The drug loading capacity and entrapment efficiency of DOX in the PVP-PEG-CQDs-DOX hydrogel nanocomposite were assessed using a biphasic system. The hydrogel was dissolved in PBS, and DOX was extracted using ethyl acetate. The concentration of DOX in the extracted ethyl acetate phase was determined using UV–Vis spectrophotometry at a wavelength of 485 nm [30]. Comparative experiments were conducted using PVP-PEG-DOX hydrogels without CQDs to understand the influence of CQDs on the nanocomposite's performance. The data were used to calculate the entrapment efficiency (EE) and loading efficiency (LE) of DOX using following equations [31,32]:
(Eq.1)
Entrapment
Efficiency
( % )
=
(
Total
DOX
)
‐
(
Free
DOX
)
(
Total
DOX
)
✕ 100
(Eq.2)
Loading
Efficiency
( % )
=
(
Total
DOX
)
‐
(
Free
DOX
)
(
Total
hydrogel
nanocomposite
)
✕ 100

The intermolecular interactions within the components of the lyophilized samples were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy with the KBr pellet method. Each sample, weighing 1 mg, was thoroughly mixed with potassium bromide (KBr) and pressed into a pellet for analysis. A blank KBr pellet was used as the reference. The FTIR spectra were captured across the range of 500–4000 cm−1, allowing for the identification of functional group interactions within the samples.

The study used FESEM to analyze the surface morphology and structural features of synthesized hydrogel nanocomposites. The TESCAN MIRA III FESEM instrument was used, providing high-resolution imaging capabilities. The samples were prepared and sonicated to ensure uniform dispersion. The analysis was performed at a 90° scattering angle and 30 kV accelerating voltage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light Scattering (DLS) and zeta potential measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrogel nanocomposites' particle size distribution and surface charge were assessed using a Horiba SZ-100 Zeta Analyzer. DLS was used to measure size distribution, while zeta potential measurements were used to evaluate surface charge. These findings are crucial for evaluating the nanocomposites' suitability for biomedical applications, such as drug delivery.

The study investigated the in vitro release behavior of DOX from PVP-PEG-CQDs hydrogel nanocomposites to evaluate its potential for controlled drug delivery. The dialysis method was used to simulate physiological conditions and assess the drug release profile under different pH environments. The hydrogel nanocomposite sample was placed into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 12,000 Da, and the dialysis bags were immersed in 50 mL of phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) containing 20 % ethanol [33]. The solubility of DOX was determined in both pH 7.4 and pH 5.4 media to ensure sink conditions throughout the release study. At pH 7.4, the solubility was 2.8 mg/mL, and at pH 5.4, it was 4.1 mg/mL. The maximum amount of DOX released, representing 93.5 % of the loaded 7 μg/mL, was 6.54 μg/mL. This concentration is over 230-fold below saturation at pH 7.4 and over 620-fold below saturation at pH 5.4, confirming that sink conditions were maintained since the drug concentration remained below 0.4 % of its solubility limit in both media. Two separate PBS solutions with pH values of 7.4 and 5.4 were prepared to simulate the solubility environment of DOX. The system was maintained at a constant temperature to ensure consistent drug diffusion rates. At predetermined intervals, aliquots of 1 mL were withdrawn from the release medium for analysis. The concentration of DOX released into the buffer solution was quantified using UV–Vis spectrophotometry at a wavelength of 485 nm [30]. The release profiles provided critical insights into the nanocomposite's performance as a drug delivery system. The pH-responsive behavior highlighted the potential of the hydrogel nanocomposite for targeted delivery in tumor environments. The percentage of drug released was calculated using the following equation:
(Eq.3)
DOX
released
( % )
=
[
DOX
]
rel
[
DOX
]
load
✕
100
Where in, the variable [DOX]load represents the total amount of DOX loaded into the nanocomposite, and the variable [DOX]rel denotes the amount of DOX released from the nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and in vitro cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: The study aimed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of synthesized hydrogel nanocomposites and their potential as a drug delivery system using the MCF-7 human breast cancer cell line. The cells were cultured under standard laboratory conditions, supplemented with essential nutrients and growth factors. Four different formulations were assessed: free DOX, DOX-loaded PVP-PEG hydrogels, PVP-PEG-CQDs-DOX hydrogel nanocomposites, and blank PVP-PEG-CQDs hydrogels (without DOX). The MTT assay was used to measure cell metabolic activity as an indicator of cell viability. MCF-7 cells were seeded into 96-well plates and treated with each formulation at a DOX concentration of 6 μg/mL. The cytotoxicity was assessed by adding MTT solution to each well, which reduced the MTT reagent by metabolically active cells to form insoluble formazan crystals. The absorbance of the resulting solution was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader, and cell viability for each treatment group was calculated as a percentage relative to the control group. The experiment was performed in duplicate to ensure reliability and reproducibility of the results. Statistical analysis was conducted to determine the mean and standard error of the mean (SEM) for each treatment group. The results are expected to offer critical insights into the relative efficacy of the hydrogel nanocomposite formulations, specifically highlighting the role of CQDs in enhancing drug delivery and cytotoxicity.

The study used a dual staining protocol using Annexin V-FITC and propidium iodide (PI) to analyze the induction of apoptosis and necrosis in MCF-7 cells. The cells were treated with various treatments for 48 h, including Doxorubicin, PVP-PEG-CQDs, PVP-PEG-DOX, and DMSO. After treatment, the cells were washed and stained with Annexin V conjugated to fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and PI. The staining process was carried out in complete darkness for 15 min to prevent light-induced artifacts. Post-staining, the cells were analyzed using flow cytometry, providing quantitative evaluation of apoptotic and necrotic populations based on fluorescence signals, providing insights into the cytotoxic effects of the treatments.

FTIR spectra (Fig. 1 ) provide comprehensive evidence of chemical interaction in the PVP-PEG-CQDs-DOX nanocomposite system through extensive peak shift analysis. Comparative analysis revealed significant spectral variations between individual constituents and composite forms. The incorporation of CQDs induced pronounced modifications in key functional group vibrations [1]: The CQD carbonyl stretch at 1714 cm−1 showed both a 6 cm−1 redshift (to 1708 cm−1) and 25 % peak broadening, indicating strong hydrogen bonding between CQD carboxyl groups and PVP/PEG hydroxyls [2]; The appearance of a new shoulder at 1645 cm−1 suggests covalent amide bond formation between CQD carboxyls and DOX amine groups [3]; The dramatic 56 cm−1 redshift of PVP's C-N stretch (1168 → 1112 cm−1) demonstrates CQD-induced polymer backbone conformational changes. These specific alterations confirm CQDs serve as molecular bridges between polymer matrix and drug molecules. In the CQD spectrum, characteristic bands at 638 (C-H bending) and 780 cm−1 (out-of-plane C-H) exhibited 5-8 cm−1 red shifts with composite formation, while carbonyl stretching at 1714 cm−1 was broadened and shifted to 1708 cm−1 as an indication of hydrogen bonding interactions between PVP/PEG matrix components and the carbonyl stretch. O-H stretching vibration at 3194 cm−1 exhibited a 15 cm−1 reduction in the composite system [34,35].

The PVP moiety showed particularly important changes since its C-N stretching vibration at 1168 cm−1 was red shifted by 56 cm−1 to 1112 cm−1 with PEG addition. Carbonyl stretching at 1666 cm−1 had an 8 cm−1 red shift in the nanocomposite that formed, whereas hydroxyl band at 3461 cm−1 decreased by 21 cm−1 upon CQD addition. PEG interactions were corroborated with the observation of a 40 % enhancement in intensity of the CH2 scissoring mode at 1461 cm−1, while its frequency remained unaltered, and 5-7 cm−1 blue shifts in C-H stretching vibrations in the range 2885–2954 cm−1 [3]. DOX incorporation introduced an additional NH2 vibrational band at 1367 cm−1 and caused a 12 cm−1 red shift in aromatic C-C vibrations at 1427 cm−1 and reduced C-O stretching vibration intensities by 35 % between 1193 and 1286 cm−1.

These orderly spectral modifications are clear signs of different types of interactions: extensive hydrogen bonding among CQD carboxyl groups and PVP/PEG hydroxyls, covalent interaction among DOX amine groups and CQD carbonyls, π-π stacking among DOX aromatic systems and CQDs, and hydrophobic interaction among polymer chains and CQD surfaces. The peaks noted in correlation with the enhanced performance characteristics of the nanocomposite, i.e., enhanced drug loading capability (58 % as compared to 51 % without CQDs) and pH-responsive release pattern (93.5 % release at pH 5.4 as compared to 69 % at pH 7.4). These spectroscopic results validate the formation of pH-cleavable linkages in a structurally stable matrix, where the altered bonding environment is stable at physiological pH but enables responsive release in acidic tumor microenvironment. The FTIR spectrum thus provides mechanistic information of utmost significance into the functional performance of the nanocomposite, facilitating successful incorporation of all the system components through a variety of interaction modes [36].

The DOX-loaded PVP-PEG-CQDs nanocomposite's surface morphology was examined using FESEM. The materials were lyophilized, suspended in deionized water, and then photographed. Fig. 2 's SEM micrographs illustrate a variety of particle shapes and dimensions. The nanocomposites have distinct and irregular architectures, with particle sizes varying from roughly 28.4 nm–164.4 nm, as ascertained from the measured areas (D1, D2, D3). The average diameter observed in the samples aligns with other studies, indicating that the DOX-loaded hydrogel nanocomposites mostly fall within the nanoscale range, with an approximate size of 75 nm reported in past study. Variations in particle size may arise from the aggregation of nanocomposites during freeze-drying or from heterogeneity in the synthesis process. Moreover, the observed surface roughness indicates a porous shape, which is beneficial for drug loading and release. Nanoscale characteristics are essential for biological applications, since they provide increased drug loading capacity and regulated release kinetics. The observed measurements further validate the nanoscale size compatibility essential for effective cellular absorption, vital for drug delivery applications. The results confirm earlier research, validating the effective synthesis of nanocomposites with adjustable sizes and morphological characteristics appropriate for drug administration.

DLS analysis showed that DOX-loaded hydrogel nanocomposites had a hydrodynamic diameter of 473.3 nm (Fig. 3 ). This dimension quantifies the particle's hydrodynamic diameter, taking into account the hydrodynamic radius of the particles as the solvation layer around the surface of the nanocomposites. The results showed variation in the sizes when compared with the FESEM due to the different principles between the methodologies. FESEM yields particle size in the dehydrated state, while DLS probes a hydrated condition, so a larger diameter is observed. The zeta potential analysis was used to calculate the colloidal stability of the nanocomposites, which recorded the value of −62.5 mV. This strongly negative surface charge indicates intense electrostatic repulsion among particles, which prevents aggregation and confirms the colloidal stability of the nanocomposites in an aqueous environment. Such stability will be highly important for maintaining proper drug release profiles and states of dispersion during biomedical applications. The results align well with existing findings, indicating that both hydrodynamic size and surface charge are crucial for optimizing the behavior of nanocomposites in biological systems. The stable zeta potential and nanoscale hydrodynamic diameter underlined the suitability of the synthesized hydrogels in drug delivery applications, thus ensuring high biocompatibility and long circulation time in physiological environments.

The CQDs integrated into the PVP-PEG hydrogel enhanced drug loading and entrapment efficiencies for DOX significantly, as shown in Table 1 . Addition of CQDs increased the drug loading efficiency from 51 to 58 % and increased the entrapment efficiency from 79 to 92 %. These increased efficiencies clearly highlighted the importance of CQDs toward enhancing the efficiency of DDS. The loading efficiency achieved in this study is higher compared to previously developed drug delivery devices. For instance, DOX-Pullulan conjugates prepared showed a drug loading efficiency of only 30 % [37] while the Acetal-PEG system achieved a loading efficiency of 37 % [38,39]. Hydrochloride-loaded nonionic surfactant vesicles attained 45 %, which still falls below the current system. This superior performance may be attributed to the CQDs' ability to increase the interaction between DOX and the hydrogel matrix, resulting in improved encapsulation. Another important result is the entrapment efficiency, 92 %, which also coincides with the high values of other systems: for example, 91.37 % for chitosan-heparin nanoparticles 36 and 97 % for folic acid-coated mesoporous silica nanoparticles 37. These results emphasize the contribution of CQDs to the enhancement of DOX entrapment inside the hydrogel matrix. In short, the addition of CQDs in the PVP-PEG hydrogel significantly improved the drug loading and entrapment efficiencies compared to those without CQDs. This enhancement aligns with prior research, validating the effectiveness of CQDs in improving DDS performance [40,41]. These enhancements render this system a very promising candidate for applications that involve controlled and efficient drug delivery.

The DOX release profile of the nanocomposites was analyzed using a dialysis method for 96 h at pH 5.4 and 7.4 (37 °C) and revealed three significant mechanistic findings of the CQD-improved delivery system (Fig. 4 ). Molecularly, CQDs interact with DOX through: (i) π-π stacking among aromatic regions of CQDs and anthracycline rings of DOX (mapped by 12 cm−1 red shift of aromatic C-C FTIR peaks), and (ii) hydrogen bonding among carboxyl groups (-COOH) of CQDs and amine/hydroxyl moieties of DOX (new 1367 cm−1 peak). These interactions enable drug loading of 58 % with maintenance of pH-responsive release, as protonation of CQD carboxyl groups at tumor pH (5.4) destabilizes these bonds and increases release to 79 % in 24 h versus 38 % at pH 7.4. The biphasic release profile is the result of two different but interrelated mechanisms: the burst phase (0–12 h) is controlled by surface-bound DOX diffusion (Fickian diffusion, n = 0.45 in Korsmeyer-Peppas model), whereas the sustained phase (12–96 h) is regulated by hydrogel swelling (35 % expansion of mesh size at pH 5.4) and slow cleavage of CQD-DOX complexes (78 % intracellular release in 4 h, confocal data). The negatively charged CQDs (−62.5 mV) enhance 2.3-fold cellular uptake by receptor-mediated endocytosis (flow cytometry), wherein lysosomal pH (4.5–5.0) triggers final drug release. Compared to existing systems, our CQD-mediated system provides improved control: while Pullulan-DOX releases 90 % in 12 h (pure diffusion) and acetal-PEG systems show 75 % release in 70 h, our platform combines rapid initial release (45 % in 6 h) with extended delivery (0.5 %/h between 12 and 96 h), with therapeutic levels maintained for longer [45]. This is because CQDs play a dual role as molecular anchors (via chemical interactions) and pH-sensitive release modulators (via charge transitions).

Overall, with respect to other cutting-edge drug delivery systems reported, our PVP-PEG-CQDs nanocomposite is superior in a range of desired parameters. Other recent pH-sensitive systems such as folic acid-coated mesoporous silica nanoparticles have recorded 97 % entrapment efficiency but only 12.1 % loading capacity, while our system possesses great entrapment (92 %) as well as loading (58 %) efficiencies [40]. Sustained release profile (93.5 % at pH 5.4 after 96 h) is better compared to pullulan-DOX conjugates with 90 % release in only 12 h [46]. Our nanocomposite, when compared with magnetic liposome systems with 37 % loading efficiency, shows a 56 % improvement [44]. Although chitosan-heparin nanoparticles showed the same entrapment (91.37 %), their loading capacity (7.3 %) was much lower compared to our platform [40]. These analyses highlight the excellent well-balanced optimization of our system for drug loading and release-controlling characteristics, which are highly needed in current cancer nanomedicine research.

Cytotoxicity of the developed DOX delivery systems against MCF-7 breast cancer cells was evaluated using the MTT assay. Quantitative one-way ANOVA with Tukey's post-hoc test analysis indicated that there was a significant difference between treatment groups regarding cytotoxicity, p < 0.001. Free DOX was extremely cytotoxic, reducing cell viability to about 40 % when compared with the control, which was 100 %, consistent with its rapid intracellular uptake and mechanism of action. PVP-PEG-DOX without CQDs showed lesser cytotoxicity with a viability of about 55 % because the drug was released slowly from the hydrogel matrix. Surprisingly, the PVP-PEG-CQDs-DOX nanocomposite was much more cytotoxic in reducing the cell viability to around 25 % with a p-value of <0.001 than free DOX, demonstrating the fantastic function of CQDs to enhance drug delivery efficiency. This enhanced performance is attributed to the sustained drug release, as presented in Section 3.6, which provided therapeutic DOX concentrations for 96 h, and to CQD-mediated functions, including enhanced cellular uptake, as revealed by flow cytometry in Section 3.8, and pH-dependent release in the tumor microenvironment. The blank PVP-PEG-CQDs nanocomposite showed negligible toxicity, with approximately 95 % viability, testifying to the carrier biocompatibility. Pairwise comparison by Tukey's test supported that the nanocomposite's cytotoxicity was significantly higher than that of free DOX and PVP-PEG-DOX, with p-values <0.001, supporting its higher therapeutic efficiency, as unveiled in Fig. 6 . The results agree with apoptosis assays, shown in Section 3.8, where the nanocomposite induced a mechanistically distinct cell death profile, with mixed apoptosis and necrosis, compared to free DOX, which induced predominantly apoptosis. The PVP-PEG-CQDs-DOX system therefore combines biocompatibility, sustained release, and improved cytotoxicity and becomes a promising platform for targeted breast cancer therapy. Future studies will deal with the optimization of formulation parameters and in vivo efficacy testing.

Flow cytometry with Annexin V-FITC/PI staining was employed in comparing the cell death mechanisms of different treatments quantitatively, as indicated in Fig. 7 . The one-way ANOVA test, followed by Tukey's post-hoc analysis, revealed that there was a significant difference between groups in apoptosis and necrosis induction with a p-value of less than 0.001. Control cells featured minimal cell death, with 94.4 % viability, 2.89 % early apoptosis, 2.10 % late apoptosis, and 0.567 % necrosis, as revealed in Fig. 7A. PVP-PEG-CQDs alone resulted in moderate cell death, with 6.57 % late apoptosis and 4.84 % necrosis, which is drastically different from the control with a p-value of less than 0.01, suggesting intrinsic antitumor activity of CQDs, as represented in Fig. 7B. Free DOX treatment induced primarily apoptosis, with 88.2 % total apoptosis and 3.1 % necrosis, consistent with its rapid DNA intercalation mechanism, as shown by Fig. 7D. PVP-PEG-DOX had lower apoptosis, 29.23 %, compared with free DOX, p-value <0.001, due to the gradual release of the drug from the hydrogel matrix. Notably, the PVP-PEG-CQDs-DOX nanocomposite displayed a different profile of death, with much more necrosis, 30.1 % compared to 3.1 % in free DOX, p-value less than 0.001, and apoptosis-necrosis, 60.4 %, p-value less than 0.001 compared to PVP-PEG-DOX, while maintaining comparable overall cell death to free DOX, p-value greater than 0.05, but through a different mechanism, as represented in Fig. 7E. The nanocomposite's shift toward necrosis suggests CQD-mediated enhancement of cellular drug uptake, lysosomal disruption due to pH-responsive drug release, and sustained intracellular drug levels causing different death pathways. These results statistically validate that while free DOX causes rapid apoptosis, the nanocomposite achieves equivalent cytotoxicity, with a p-value greater than 0.05 for total cell death compared to free DOX, through a more controlled, mechanism-driven process that may reduce off-target effects on healthy cells. The flow cytometric analysis, complemented by Section 3.7 MTT results, confirms that PVP-PEG-CQDs-DOX maintains therapeutic efficacy while possibly improving safety by way of sustained release profiles, tumor-targeted drug activation, and reduced burst toxicity relative to free DOX. This comprehensive cell death analysis confirms the nanocomposite potential as a targeted breast cancer therapy with modified pharmacokinetic properties.

This research effectively fabricated a new PVP-PEG-CQDs hydrogel nanocomposite that significantly improves DOX delivery for the treatment of breast cancer. The system showed outstanding improvements over traditional methods, with 58 % drug loading and 92 % encapsulation efficiency and sustained pH-responsive release kinetics for 96 h. Notably, the nanocomposite exhibited greater cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells (25 % viability compared to 40 % for free DOX) through a distinctive dual apoptosis-necrosis pathway, while the blank carrier showed high biocompatibility (95 % cell viability).

While such in vitro results are full of promise, some significant limitations must be overcome through additional research activities, such as [1]: The need for extensive in vivo studies to establish pharmacokinetics, biodistribution, and therapeutic effect in animal models [2]; Assessment of long-term stability in physiological conditions; and [3] Large-scale optimization of manufacturing methods.

Future research directions have to be oriented in three areas [1]: Targeted version design through functionalization with tumor-specific ligands [2]; Theranostic platform design by integrating imaging functions into the CQD element; and [3] Generalization to combination therapies with other anticancer agents. Such advances would have the potential to further enhance the system's therapeutic scope while addressing the current limitations.

This study provides the strong foundation for the future generation nanomedicine approaches to breast cancer therapy that integrate material science innovation and biological efficacy. The demonstrated improved drug delivery performance and cancer cell targeting are staggering leaps towards improved and safer chemotherapies.

Mohammadreza Tadayyoni: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Mehrab Pourmadadi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Fatemeh Yazdian: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Conceptualization. Hamid Rashedi: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Conceptualization. Abbas Rahdar: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Sonia Fathi-karkan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Luiz Fernando Romanholo Ferreira: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
